findings.description:
[NVD] Caddy's HTTP `path` request matcher is intended to be case-insensitive, but when the match pattern contains percent-escape sequences (`%xx`) it compares against the request's escaped path without lowercasing. An attacker can bypass path-based routing and any access controls attached to that route by changing the casing of the request path.

[Task Description]
Caddy is an extensible server platform that uses TLS by default. Prior to version 2.11.1, Caddy's HTTP `path` request matcher is intended to be case-insensitive, but when the match pattern contains percent-escape sequences (`%xx`) it compares against the request's escaped path without lowercasing. An attacker can bypass path-based routing and any access controls attached to that route by changing the casing of the request path. Version 2.11.1 contains a fix for the issue.
findings.recommendation: This finding is a **TRUE POSITIVE**. Percent-encoded path matchers were lower-cased during provisioning, but the request path was compared in its raw escaped form without normalization, letting uppercase escape sequences bypass protected routes. The fix lower-cases the cleaned escaped path whenever a matcher contains `%`, restoring the intended case-insensitive comparison even in raw path space.
findings.vuln_title: CVE-2026-27587: caddy Broken Function Level Authorization

dataflow.description: Caddyfile config token starting with '/' is converted into a MatchPath matcher. The pattern (e.g., '/admin/%2Fsecret') is lowercased during Provision (matchers.go:412), establishing the intended case-insensitive contract.
MatchPath.Provision lowercases every configured path pattern via strings.ToLower (line 412), establishing the design intent of case-insensitive matching.
Route.Provision calls ProvisionMatchers which loads MatchPath modules from config via ctx.LoadModule, wiring the matchers into the route's MatcherSets.
External HTTP request arrives at wrapRoute's HandlerFunc. The *http.Request carries the attacker-controlled URL path in both r.URL.Path (decoded) and r.URL.EscapedPath() (raw/escaped).
route.MatcherSets.AnyMatchWithError(req) is called to evaluate whether the request matches this route's matchers. Delegates to MatchPath.MatchWithError.
MatchPath.MatchWithError receives the *http.Request. At line 431, reqPath := strings.ToLower(r.URL.Path) lowercases the decoded path — the normal code path is properly case-insensitive.

When the match pattern contains '%' (e.g., '/admin/%2Fsecret'), the code switches to r.URL.EscapedPath() — the raw/escaped form — and passes it through CleanPath but WITHOUT calling strings.ToLower. This is the root cause: the escaped path retains the attacker's original casing.
matchPatternWithEscapeSequence iterates the escaped path character-by-character. At line 556, pathCh := string(escapedPath[iPath]) reads each raw byte, preserving the attacker's casing.

In the default (non-%, non-*) case, sb.WriteString(pathCh) appends the character with its original casing into the comparison string. Percent-escaped sequences get lowercased (line 567), but ordinary characters like 'A'-'Z' do not.

path.Match(matchPath, sb.String()) performs the final glob comparison. Since matchPath was lowercased during Provision but sb.String() retains the attacker's mixed case, a request like '/Admin/%2fsecret' fails to match the provisioned '/admin/%2fsecret' — the comparison is case-sensitive.
MatchWithError returns false — the matcher did not match because of the casing discrepancy.

Since !matches, the route is skipped entirely: return nextCopy.ServeHTTP(rw, req). All handlers attached to this route — including authentication middleware (e.g., HTTPBasicAuth at caddyauth/basicauth.go:142) — are bypassed. The attacker reaches the next route or falls through to an unprotected handler.

findings.description:
[NVD] Caddy's HTTP `host` request matcher is documented as case-insensitive, but when configured with a large host list (>100 entries) it becomes case-sensitive due to an optimized matching path. An attacker can bypass host-based routing and any access controls attached to that route by changing the casing of the `Host` header.

[Task Description]
Caddy is an extensible server platform that uses TLS by default. Prior to version 2.11.1, Caddy's HTTP `host` request matcher is documented as case-insensitive, but when configured with a large host list (>100 entries) it becomes case-sensitive due to an optimized matching path. An attacker can bypass host-based routing and any access controls attached to that route by changing the casing of the `Host` header. Version 2.11.1 contains a fix for the issue.
findings.recommendation: This finding is a **TRUE POSITIVE**. Host lists exceeding 100 entries were sorted with their original casing, so the binary search looked up `Host` headers case-sensitively and allowed bypassing route protections by altering letter case. The fix lower-cases every exact host entry during provisioning and performs the binary search against the lower-cased request host so comparisons stay case-insensitive even for large lists.

dataflow.description: Attacker-controlled Host header (r.Host) is extracted via net.SplitHostPort into reqHost. No lower-casing or normalization is applied to reqHost.

In Provision(), host entries are stored with original casing: only ASCII conversion (idna.ToASCII) is applied, but strings.ToLower at line 268 is used only for duplicate detection in the 'seen' map, not for storing back to m[i]. Exact hosts retain mixed-case values.

m.large() check routes to the binary-search fast path when the host list exceeds 100 entries (defined at line 394). For small lists, a linear scan with strings.EqualFold handles case-insensitivity correctly.
sort.Search performs binary search using case-sensitive comparison: m[i] >= reqHost. Neither m[i] nor reqHost is lower-cased, so the inequality comparison is case-sensitive.

The exact-match check m[pos] == reqHost uses direct string equality (case-sensitive). When the attacker changes Host header casing (e.g., 'ADMIN.example.com' vs 'admin.example.com'), the binary search fails to find the match.

Fallback linear loop breaks immediately for non-fuzzy (exact) hosts when m.large() is true, preventing strings.EqualFold from ever executing for exact entries. Only wildcard/placeholder hosts reach the loop body.
wrapRoute calls route.MatcherSets.AnyMatchWithError(req). The host matcher returns false due to casing mismatch.
When matches is false, the route is skipped and the request falls through to nextCopy.ServeHTTP — bypassing host-based routing and any access controls attached to the intended route.

findings.description:
[NVD] Caddy's `forward_auth` directive with `copy_headers` generates conditional header-set operations that only fire when the upstream auth service includes the named header in its response. No delete or remove operation is generated for the original client-supplied request header with the same name.

When an auth service returns `200 OK` without one of the configured `copy_headers` headers, the client-supplied header passes through unchanged to the backend. Any requester holding a valid authentication token can inject arbitrary values for trusted identity headers, resulting in privilege escalation.

This is a regression introduced by PR #6608 in November 2024. All stable releases from v2.10.0 onward are affected.

---

[Task Description]
Caddy is an extensible server platform that uses TLS by default. From version 2.10.0 to before version 2.11.2, forward_auth copy_headers does not strip client-supplied headers, allowing identity injection and privilege escalation. This issue has been patched in version 2.11.2.
findings.recommendation:
This finding is a **TRUE POSITIVE**. The forward auth shortcut let client-controlled identity headers (for example `Remote-User`) flow through untouched whenever the upstream auth service omitted them, so backends trusted spoofed roles. The fix canonicalizes header mappings, removes the attacker-controlled copies before copying trusted values, and updates the regression test to assert the sanitized behavior.

`modules/caddyhttp/reverseproxy/forwardauth/caddyfile.go`

`verify_requirements/tests/forward_auth_bfla_test.go`
findings.vuln_title: CVE-2026-30851: caddy Broken Function Level Authorization

dataflow.description: User Caddyfile 'copy_headers' directive parsed: attacker-controlled header names (e.g., Remote-User, Remote-Email) are read from config and stored into headersToCopy map. This defines which headers the auth service is trusted to provide — but no corresponding Delete is configured to strip the client-supplied versions first.
Headers to copy are sorted for deterministic output; the sorted list drives the route generation loop.

copyHeaderRoutes generation begins. For each header in headersToCopy, a route is created with a headers.Handler that only calls Set (no Delete). The route uses a MatchNot wrapper with a vars matcher that skips when the placeholder is empty — meaning if the auth service omits the header, the client's original value survives unchanged.

headers.Handler constructed with Request.HeaderOps containing only Set operations. The Set value references {http.reverse_proxy.header.<Header>} placeholder sourced from the auth response. No Delete operation is configured to strip the client-supplied header before Set.
Route appended to copyHeaderRoutes with MatchNot+vars guard: if the auth response placeholder is empty, the entire Set handler is skipped — leaving the client's spoofed header intact.

At runtime, after the reverse proxy receives the auth service response, response headers are stored as replacer placeholders: repl.Set('http.reverse_proxy.header.'+field, value). If the auth service does not include a given header, the placeholder remains unset/empty.
The copyHeaderRoutes are appended to goodResponseHandler.Routes. These routes execute in the handle_response handler chain for 2xx auth responses.

headers.Handler.ServeHTTP applies Request HeaderOps (Set only) to the original request. When the route is skipped (placeholder empty), this handler never runs; when it runs, it only Sets — never Deletes the pre-existing client header value.

VULNERABILITY MANIFESTS: The copyHeaderRoutes lack a preceding Delete operation. When the auth service omits a header (e.g., Remote-User) from its 2xx response, the placeholder is empty, the vars matcher matches (value is ''), MatchNot skips the route entirely, and the client-supplied spoofed header (e.g., 'Remote-User: spoofed-admin') survives untouched through the request chain to the backend application — enabling identity injection and privilege escalation (BFLA/CWE-285).
